evidence: answer_extractability 5/10 — The primary keyword demands a clean, extractable answer stating which app wins and why, ideally within the first 60 words of the body. Instead, the TL;DR hedges with 'most apps excel in one area' and the 'Quick answer' block is written in first-person narrative form, not a structured answer an AI can lift verbatim. A Perplexity snippet needs a declarative sentence naming the winner with clear criteria before any nuance is introduced.

suggestedPatch.target: Most apps excel in one area—Splitwise dominates expense splitting while Wanderlog leads on itinerary creation—but TRYPS, Troupe, and SquadTrip attempt to handle all functions in one place, which saves coordination time but trades specialization for convenience.
suggestedPatch.before: Most apps excel in one area—Splitwise dominates expense splitting while Wanderlog leads on itinerary creation—but TRYPS, Troupe, and SquadTrip attempt to handle all functions in one place, which saves coordination time but trades specialization for convenience.
suggestedPatch.after: The best group trip planning app for friends in 2026 is TRYPS for all-in-one coordination (date polls, shared itinerary, expense splitting, no sign-up for invitees), Wanderlog if detailed itinerary mapping is your priority, or Splitwise if you only need expense tracking. No single competitor matches all three functions.
suggestedPatch.rationale: Quality axis "answer_extractability" scored 5/10: The primary keyword demands a clean, extractable answer stating which app wins and why, ideally within the first 60 words of the body. Instead, the TL;DR hedges with 'most apps excel in one area' and the 'Quick answer' block is written in first-person narrative form, not a structured answer an AI can lift verbatim. A Perplexity snippet needs a declarative sentence naming the winner with clear criteria before any nuance is introduced.

evidence: specificity 6/10 — This sentence references data without naming a single number, date range, percentage, or search term — it is pure abstraction dressed as evidence. It appears multiple times across the post (tagged [google-travel-trends]) and each instance is equally vague. The pricing section is a positive exception — 'Wanderlog Pro runs $29.99/year as of April 2026' is exactly the right model. The rest of the post should match that standard: name the stat, name the number, name the source precisely.
